source.title: Storage Tank Fire at Intercontinental Terminals Company, LLC (ITC) Terminal

actions_taken: Emergency Response Team activated and responded to tank farm
Plant-wide alarm activated to alert on-site personnel
Fixed fire monitors deployed to fight fire
Shelter-in-place orders issued for surrounding communities
Houston Ship Channel closed for seven miles
Waterfront parks closed in Harris County and City of LaPorte
Firefighting operations continued for three days until March 20, 2019
event.summary: On March 17, 2019, a mechanical seal failure on the Tank 80-8 circulation pump resulted in release of approximately 177+ barrels of butane-enriched naphtha product over approximately 30 minutes. The released flammable vapors accumulated undetected in the absence of gas detection systems and ignited at 10:00:46 a.m. The resulting fire spread to 14 adjacent tanks in the First & Second 80's tank farm due to tank spacing, design, and lack of remotely operated isolation valves. The fire burned for three days, destroying 15 tanks and their contents. A containment wall breach released an estimated 470,000–523,000 barrels of hydrocarbon products, foam, and contaminated water into Tucker Bayou, Buffalo Bayou, and the Houston Ship Channel. The incident caused no fatalities or injuries but resulted in significant environmental contamination, community disruption, and over $150 million in property damage.
recommendations: Develop and implement a process safety management system for all atmospheric storage tanks and associated equipment in highly hazardous chemical service at ITC Deer Park terminal
Develop and implement a condition monitoring program for all pumps in highly hazardous chemical service with vibration alarms per ANSI/HI 9.6.9-2018
Install flammable gas detection systems with alarm functions in product storage and transfer areas where flammable substance releases could occur

Install remotely operated emergency isolation valves configured to Fail-Closed for all atmospheric storage tanks containing highly hazardous chemicals or liquids with NFPA-3 or higher flammability rating
Conduct evaluation of tank farm design against API STD 2610 and NFPA 30 standards, addressing tank spacing, subdivisions, process equipment placement, and drainage systems
Update API STD 2610 to include flammable gas detection systems in leak detection section
Eliminate the atmospheric storage tank exemption from OSHA PSM standard
Modify EPA RMP rule to expand coverage to include all flammable liquids with NFPA-3 or higher flammability rating

threats.name: Mechanical seal failure on circulation pump

consequences.name: Release of butane-enriched naphtha product to atmosphere
Community air quality impact and shelter-in-place orders

controls.name: Mechanical Integrity Program for Pumps
controls.barrier_role: Prevent pump seal failure through condition monitoring and maintenance
controls.lod_basis: Formal mechanical integrity procedures required by PSM standards

supporting_text: ITC did not have a formal mechanical integrity procedure in place that defined requirements for maintaining the mechanical integrity of Tank 80-8 and its associated equipment, including the Tank 80-8 circulation pump

A formal mechanical integrity program for pumps in highly hazardous chemical service could have prevented this incident by providing ITC with additional opportunities to identify pump issues prior to the incident
The mechanical seal on the pump failed on March 17, 2019, allowing butane-enriched naphtha product to release from the pump while it continued to operate

controls.name: Flammable Gas Detection System
controls.barrier_role: Detect flammable vapor accumulation and alert operators before ignition
controls.lod_basis: Recommended by 2014 hazard review team; industry best practice for flammable liquid storage areas

supporting_text: Tank 80-8 was not equipped with a flammable gas detection system to warn personnel of a hazardous atmosphere resulting from loss of containment from the tank or its associated equipment

In 2014, a hazard review team recommended the addition of flammable gas detection systems near Tank 80-8; however, ITC did not implement this recommendation, and did not document why it was not implemented
In the absence of a flammable gas detection system, there were no alarms to alert personnel about the initial release of butane-enriched naphtha product around the Tank 80-8 piping manifold
The butane-enriched naphtha product continued to release from the failed pump for approximately 30 minutes, completely undetected, before its flammable vapors eventually ignited
The First & Second 80's tank farm was not equipped with a gas detection system

controls.name: Remotely Operated Emergency Isolation Valves (ROEIVs)
controls.barrier_role: Isolate and stop release of flammable liquid from tank and piping system during emergency
controls.lod_basis: Industry best practice for hazardous chemical storage; identified through PSM/RMP hazard assessments

supporting_text: Tank 80-8 and the other aboveground storage tanks located in the First & Second 80's tank farm were not equipped with remotely operated emergency isolation valves (ROEIVs) designed to mitigate process releases remotely from a safe location

On the day of the incident, the large volume of butane-enriched naphtha product contained in Tank 80-8 could not be remotely or automatically isolated, and it continued to release, via the failed pump, fueling the fire that continued to intensify around the tank

As the Tank 80-8 fire intensified, flames from the fire spread to adjacent tank piping manifolds in the tank farm and eventually compromised the equipment, causing breaches in piping that allowed the hydrocarbon and petrochemical products contained in the storage tanks to release into the common containment area

controls.name: Tank Farm Design and Layout
controls.barrier_role: Prevent fire spread between tanks through adequate spacing and subdivisions
controls.lod_basis: NFPA 30 and API STD 2610 standards for tank farm design

supporting_text: Although the First & Second 80's tank farm was designed largely in accordance with applicable National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) 30 requirements, elements of the tank farm design, including tank spacing, subdivisions, engineering controls for pumps located inside the containment area, and drainage systems, made it difficult for emergency responders to slow or prevent the spread of the initial fire and allowed the fire to spread to other tanks within the tank farm

Elements of the tank farm design, including tank spacing, subdivisions, engineering controls for pumps located inside the containment area, and drainage systems also contributed to the severity of the incident by allowing the fire to spread to other tanks within the tank farm

controls.name: Fixed Fire Monitors
controls.barrier_role: Suppress fire at source using water spray
controls.lod_basis: Standard fire protection equipment for tank farms

supporting_text: The first responding member of the ERT entered the tank farm near Tank 80-12 and attempted to activate the fixed fire monitor located in the center of Tanks 80-8, 80-9, 80-11, and 80-12. However, the fire monitor was engulfed in flames, and he was unable to access or activate it

He then approached the fixed fire monitor between Tanks 80-7, 80-8, 80-10, and 80-11, directed it towards the fire at the Tank 80-8 piping manifold, and opened the valve to start the flow of water. The acting shift manager described the flow of water coming out of this monitor as a 'trickle' not capable of reaching the fire
The positions of these fixed fire monitors did not allow for them to be aimed directly at the fire engulfing the Tank 80-8 piping manifold, as they were not designed to reach that area

controls.name: Distributed Control System (DCS) Monitoring and Alarms
controls.barrier_role: Detect abnormal operating conditions and alert operators to take corrective action
controls.lod_basis: Standard process control system for tank operations

supporting_text: Beginning at approximately 7:25 a.m., the pump discharge pressure began to increase with no change in operation having occurred
The unanticipated changes in the computer control system data did not trigger any alarms in the central control room (CCR) since the changes did not reach any applicable alarm setpoints
The Hi and Lo alarm setpoints for the Tank 80-8 pump discharge pressure were 130 and 0 psi, respectively

The CCR operator on duty was not actively monitoring Tank 80-8 because no loading or unloading activity was ongoing at the time. As a result, the CCR operator did not identify the ongoing reduction in tank volume and took no measures to secure the butane-enriched naphtha product release

controls.name: Process Safety Management (PSM) Program
controls.barrier_role: Systematically identify and control hazards through hazard analysis, mechanical integrity, and other PSM elements

supporting_text: ITC did not apply a formal process safety management program to Tank 80-8 because neither the OSHA PSM standard nor the EPA RMP rule applied to Tank 80-8 and its associated equipment

Tank 80-8 was not covered by the OSHA PSM standard due to the atmospheric storage tank exemption in the standard, and the EPA RMP rule did not apply due to the flammability rating exemption in the rule for the butane-enriched naphtha mixture

Although ITC applied some process safety management elements across the terminal, the company did not apply other key elements, such as Mechanical Integrity and Process Hazard Analysis, to atmospheric storage tanks in highly hazardous chemical service

Had ITC developed and implemented a comprehensive process safety management program that effectively identified and controlled hazards for Tank 80-8 and its related equipment, the incident could have been prevented

controls.name: Secondary Containment System
controls.barrier_role: Contain released liquids and prevent environmental contamination
controls.lod_basis: Standard environmental protection requirement for tank farms

supporting_text: A containment wall around the tanks breached and released an estimated 470,000–523,000 barrels of hydrocarbon and petrochemical products, firefighting aqueous film forming foam, and contaminated water into Tucker Bayou and adjacent water, sediments, and habitats

The resulting accumulation of hydrocarbon and petrochemical products, firefighting foam, and contaminated water in the secondary containment area ultimately contributed to a breach of the containment wall and a release of materials to the local waterways

controls.name: Management of Change (MOC) Process
controls.barrier_role: Evaluate and document changes to equipment and processes to identify new hazards
controls.lod_basis: PSM element applied by ITC across terminal

supporting_text: Although the OSHA PSM standard and EPA RMP rule did not apply to Tank 80-8, ITC used its management of change (MOC) process to document the Tank 80-8 Truck Butane Injection System project
As part of this MOC, ITC completed a pre-startup safety review (PSSR) for the project on August 18, 2014

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
